schools.summary: The opening of the calendar of holy seasons — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (the feasts not of human devising but of God's appointing, received much about the same time as the foregoing laws of holiness, Gill); only God's appointment can make time holy, he being the Lord of time who sanctifies what portions he will, the holy days drawing holiness from his word not the day itself (Henry); the order of Leviticus — having regulated the holiness of sanctuary, sacrifices, priests, and people, the law now turns to the holiness of seasons (Ellicott); a preview of the feasts to come — Sabbath, Passover and unleavened bread, firstfruits, Pentecost, trumpets, atonement, tabernacles (Clarke); God ordering not only how but when his people worship.

The feasts as God's, proclaimed as holy convocations — 'even these are my feasts' (the LORD claiming them as his own, set apart for his honour, God's appointments not the people's inventions); the term moed, an appointed solemn season used of any anniversary recording great ecclesiastical or providential facts, fixed times for remembering God's acts (Clarke); 'ye shall proclaim' (done by the priests, who announced the feasts, the people summoned, Wesley); 'holy convocations' (days for the people to assemble to God's worship in a special manner, corporate called gatherings of the whole congregation, Wesley/Ellicott); the feasts both holy and convocations, worship appointed, public, and communal.

The weekly Sabbath set first — 'six days shall work be done: but the seventh day is the sabbath of rest' (the ordinary labour of six days bounded by the holy rest of the seventh, the pattern of creation, Gen 2:3); the Sabbath, as the first and greatest solemnity, named before all the yearly feasts, he who would not keep this not fit to keep any other, the weekly rest the school of all the rest (Clarke); the oldest and most important festival, recurring every week, fitly at the threshold of the holy seasons (Ellicott); 'no work therein' excluding all earthly employment of profit or pleasure, the rest entire (Wesley); 'in all your dwellings' — kept in every house throughout the land, not only at the sanctuary.

themes: six days shall work be done
the weekly rest heads the calendar

schools.summary: The heading of the yearly feasts — 'these are the feasts of the LORD... which ye shall proclaim in their seasons' (a fresh heading over the yearly feasts, distinct from the weekly Sabbath); called his because he appointed them, set against Jeroboam who devised a feast of his own heart, an affront to God, 1 Kgs 12:33 — will-worship no true feast of the LORD (Henry); 'in their seasons,' at their appointed time, each feast fixed in the year, kept neither sooner nor later (Keil and Delitzsch); introducing the festivals proper, distinguished from the weekly Sabbath, in regular order (Ellicott); all these festivals having an affinity to the Sabbath, partaking of its holy rest, as greater Sabbaths (Calvin).

The Passover, first of the yearly feasts — 'the fourteenth day of the first month at even is the LORD's passover' (the month called Abib in the Pentateuch and Nisan in the later Scriptures, the month of the Exodus made the first of the year, Ex 12:2, Ellicott/Gill); a brief restatement, the leading directions repeated from Exodus 12:6ff where the full institution was given (Keil and Delitzsch, Clarke); the Passover at the head of the year's feasts, redemption from Egypt the foundation of Israel's life; the typology carried from Exodus — the lamb prefiguring Christ, 'our passover sacrificed for us' (1 Cor 5:7), the year opening with the blood of the Lamb.

themes: the fourteenth day of the first month

schools.summary: The feast of unleavened bread — 'on the fifteenth day of the same month... seven days ye must eat unleavened bread' (the feast opening the morning after the paschal evening, running seven days); the fifteenth the very day Israel went out of Egypt carrying their dough unleavened, having no time to leaven it in their haste, the feast commemorating that departure (Gill); the directions given fully in Exodus 12:15ff (Ellicott); the typology — leaven, which spreads and corrupts, a figure of sin and the 'old leaven' of malice, to be purged, keeping the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Cor 5:8); redemption (Passover) issuing at once in holiness (unleavened bread), the people saved by blood living a purged life.

The first day's holy convocation — 'in the first day ye shall have an holy convocation' (the fifteenth of Nisan distinguished from the other days as a day for the people called together to God's worship, Gill); 'ye shall do no servile work therein' (not the entire cessation of the Sabbath and atonement, but abstinence from servile or laborious work, defined during the second Temple as building, pulling down, and heavy toil, the necessary preparing of food allowed, Ex 12:16, Ellicott); the distinction between festival days, on which servile work alone was forbidden, and the Sabbath and atonement, on which no work at all; the week of holiness opening and closing with a sacred assembly, the joyful days framed by worship.

The seven days' offerings and the seventh-day convocation — 'ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD seven days' (on every one of the seven days, besides the continual daily sacrifice, a special burnt offering — two young bullocks, a ram, seven lambs, with meat and drink offerings, and a goat for a sin offering, Num 28:19, Ellicott/Gill); the feast not idle rejoicing but a week of worship sustained by sacrifice; 'in the seventh day is an holy convocation' (the last day, like the first, a sacred assembly with rest from servile labour, the week framed at both ends by holy days); the daily sin offering keeping the people mindful that even their feasts needed atonement, joy and the sense of sin held together.

themes: an offering made by fire seven days
the seventh day an holy convocation

schools.summary: The new word introducing the firstfruits — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (the repeated preface marking off the firstfruits as a distinct communication, not a continuation of the unleavened-bread law); why it stands apart — the sheaf of firstfruits formed no part of the original Passover institution in Egypt (Ex 12:1), belonging to harvest, and the omer ritual could not be kept in the wilderness where there was no reaping, given prospectively against the time they should come into the land (Ellicott); the law forward-looking, God ordaining beforehand how Israel shall acknowledge him as the giver of the harvest; the typological weight — the sheaf prefiguring Christ risen, 'the firstfruits of them that slept' (1 Cor 15:20).

The sheaf of firstfruits — 'when ye be come into the land... then ye shall bring a sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest unto the priest' (a law given prospectively, binding when they should possess and till the land, Lev 19:23, Ellicott); the first ripe grain (barley, the earliest crop) brought to the priest, not in the ear or a rough handful but purged from the chaff, dried, and beaten out, an omer of prepared corn (Lev 2:14, Wesley); the rite on the day after the first holy convocation, dedicated to the firstfruits, delivered into the priest's hand (Calvin); the principle — God the giver acknowledged with the first and best before the people take their own use, the firstfruits hallowing the whole crop; the typology — the sheaf prefiguring Christ, 'the firstfruits of them that slept' (1 Cor 15:20).

themes: when ye be come into the land
the first of the harvest to God

schools.summary: The waving of the sheaf — 'he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD, to be accepted for you' (the priest moving it to and fro before the people, calling attention to God's providence in preserving the fruits of the earth, stirring gratitude, Clarke); the manner — the omer of meal mixed with a log of oil and a handful of frankincense, waved, a handful burnt on the altar, Lev 2:15 (Ellicott); the representative character — waved in the name of the whole congregation, the one sheaf sanctifying the entire harvest and giving comfortable use of the rest, the part hallowing the whole (Wesley, Gill); the typology — the sheaf 'accepted for you' prefiguring Christ risen and presented before God, accepted for his people, the firstfruits guaranteeing their harvest (1 Cor 15:23).

The burnt offering with the sheaf — 'ye shall offer that day when ye wave the sheaf an he lamb without blemish of the first year for a burnt offering' (on the very day the sheaf was waved, a perfect male lamb offered whole, over and above the daily morning and evening sacrifice and the special offerings of the seven days, Lev 23:8, Ellicott/Gill); the fitness — the burnt offering, wholly consumed and ascending to God, expressing entire consecration and thanksgiving, the people giving back not only the firstfruits but a whole victim; 'without blemish... of the first year' (sound and unflawed, fit for the altar); the typology — the spotless lamb pointing to Christ, through whom the firstfruits and harvest are accepted; gratitude clothed in blood.

themes: a burnt offering unto the LORD

schools.summary: The doubled meat offering — 'two tenth deals of fine flour mingled with oil... the drink offering of wine, the fourth part of an hin' (ordinarily only one tenth deal accompanied a lamb, Num 28:9, but here doubled, Ellicott); the doubling exhibiting the plentifulness of the harvest now begun, the increased meat offering owning the increased bounty of God; one tenth deal the ordinary measure due with the lamb, the second added in special acknowledgment of the harvest, the quantity itself preaching gratitude (Wesley); the firstfruits offering gathering grain, oil, and wine — the staple fruits of the land — into one act of thanksgiving, all given back to God; the increase of the offering matching the increase of the field.

themes: two tenth deals of fine flour

schools.summary: The firstfruits given before the harvest is used — 'ye shall eat neither bread, nor parched corn, nor green ears, until the selfsame day that ye have brought an offering unto your God' (until the firstfruits sheaf was offered, none might eat of the new grain in any form — bread, parched, or green from the ear, the common ways of eating new corn, Gill/Wesley); God's portion coming first; the principle — it is right that God, the dispenser of every blessing and giver of the harvest, be acknowledged with the firstfruits before his creatures take their own enjoyment, to eat first being to forget the giver (Clarke, Ellicott); the discipline of patience and reverence; the typology — as the firstfruits before the harvest's use, so Christ the firstfruits presented before his people enter their inheritance.

themes: eat neither bread nor parched corn nor green ears
until ye have brought an offering

schools.summary: The counting toward Pentecost — 'ye shall count... seven sabbaths shall be complete' (seven full weeks, forty-nine days, numbered from the wave sheaf, the fiftieth day the feast of weeks); the names — feast of weeks, of harvest, and in the NT Pentecost from the fifty days (Henry); the careful deliberate counting from firstfruits to the full harvest feast; the typology — from the firstfruits (Christ's resurrection) fifty days counted to Pentecost when the Spirit was poured out (Acts 2:1), the wave sheaf and the loaves linked as resurrection and the gift of the Spirit.

debates.question: Does 'the morrow after the sabbath' mean the day after the weekly Sabbath, or after the first day of unleavened bread?

sides.position: after the first day of unleavened bread (the fifteenth of Nisan, kept as a sabbath/rest-day) — so the count begins on the sixteenth; the Pharisaic and traditional reading
after the weekly Sabbath in Passover week (so always beginning on the first day of the week) — the Sadducean/Boethusian reading, implied but not adopted by the witnesses

themes: count from the morrow after the sabbath

schools.summary: The fiftieth day and the new offering — 'even unto the morrow after the seventh sabbath shall ye number fifty days' (after seven complete weeks, forty-nine days, from the wave sheaf, the next day the fiftieth the feast, hence its NT name Pentecost, the 'fiftieth-day' feast, Acts 2:1, Ellicott); 'a new meat offering unto the LORD' (a new meal offering of the new corn baked into loaves, Wesley; the firstfruits sheaf at Passover of barley, the firstfruits at Pentecost of the later wheat harvest); the symmetry — the sheaf opening the harvest and the two loaves closing it, the whole grain harvest framed by thanksgiving; the typology — the fiftieth day figuring the Pentecost of the Spirit (Acts 2:1), the loaves the church presented as firstfruits of the new creation.

themes: the morrow after the seventh sabbath

schools.summary: The two leavened loaves — 'ye shall bring out of your habitations two wave loaves... they are the firstfruits unto the LORD' (from their own dwellings in Canaan, of home-grown grain not foreign, two loaves of fine flour, the firstfruits of the wheat harvest, waved before the LORD, Gill/Ellicott); the striking detail 'they shall be baken with leaven' — whereas leaven was generally excluded from offerings by fire (Lev 2:11), these Pentecost loaves expressly leavened, representing not the unleavened bread of the Exodus haste but the ordinary daily bread of the people's tables, now offered to God; being leavened, waved and given to the priests but not burnt on the altar; the typology — the two leavened loaves figuring the church, real firstfruits yet not sinless, accepted through the sacrifices offered with them.

themes: the bread of the people offered

schools.summary: The sacrifices with the loaves — 'seven lambs without blemish of the first year, and one young bullock, and two rams... for a burnt offering' (offered with the bread, with their meat and drink offerings, a sweet savour); the apparent discrepancy with Numbers — here one bullock and two rams, while Num 28:27 names two bullocks and one ram, resolved by supposing some liberty allowed, or more likely that the two passages name complementary sets proper to the day, the totals two bullocks, one ram, seven lambs, with a goat for a sin offering (Wesley, Ellicott); the loaves, being leavened, could not be burnt, so a full complement of unblemished burnt offerings added, the firstfruits presented with atoning and consecrating blood; the spotless victims pointing to Christ.

themes: one young bullock, and two rams
a burnt offering with the bread

schools.summary: The sin and peace offerings — 'one kid of the goats for a sin offering, and two lambs of the first year for a sacrifice of peace offerings'; the sin offering for the sin of the whole congregation, typical of Christ whose soul was made an offering for sin, in virtue of whom all other offerings are accepted (Gill); a kid here, where Lev 4:14 required a bullock — the bullock for some particular gross sin, this kid a general sin offering proper to the feast (Wesley); the peace offerings, of which the offerers partook, expressing fellowship and communion with God; the fulness of the day's worship — burnt offerings of consecration, a sin offering of atonement, peace offerings of communion, all with the loaves; even at the joyful feast atonement not omitted; the typology — Christ at once burnt offering, sin offering, and peace.

themes: one kid of the goats for a sin offering
all the sacrifices meet in Christ

schools.summary: The waving and the priest's portion — 'the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits for a wave offering before the LORD' (the two loaves and the two peace-offering lambs presented together with the waving ceremony, in the second Temple the lambs waved while yet alive with the bread, Ellicott); some part or the offerings in token waved in the name of the whole congregation, the loaves and lambs too large to be literally moved (Wesley); 'they shall be holy to the LORD for the priest' (once waved, given to the priests for their portion, the priest receiving more than his usual share of peace offerings, Wesley); the firstfruits dedicated to God assigned to those who ministered before him, the priests living from the offerings (1 Cor 9:13).

themes: with the bread of the firstfruits
holy to the LORD for the priest
the priest fed from the offerings

schools.summary: Pentecost a holy convocation — 'ye shall proclaim on the selfsame day, that it may be an holy convocation' (announced to the people by the priests with trumpet blasts, the fiftieth day a sacred assembly, Gill/Ellicott/Keil); 'no servile work therein' (laborious work laid aside, the needful preparing of food allowed, the day freed for worship); Pentecost held to commemorate the giving of the law at Sinai about fifty days after the Exodus, joining the firstfruits of the field to the founding of the covenant nation (Wesley); 'a statute for ever in all your dwellings'; the typology — fulfilled in the Christian Pentecost, fifty days after the resurrection the Spirit given and the church gathered as God's firstfruits (Acts 2:1).

themes: the harvest of grain and of souls

schools.summary: The gleaning left for the poor — 'thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field... thou shalt leave them unto the poor, and to the stranger' (the reaper not to reap the very edges nor gather the dropped gleanings, but leave them for the needy and the sojourner, the same law as Lev 19:9, Clarke/Keil); the fitness of its repetition at the feast of weeks, the firstfruits of wheat, when the people rejoiced in God's bounty they were the more to remember the poor, charity set in the midst of thankfulness (Gill, Ellicott); the change from plural 'ye reap' to singular 'thou shalt,' pressing the duty on every individual reaper that none plead exemption (Wesley); those blessed with plenty stewards for the needy, the harvest not wholly their own; true thanksgiving overflowing in mercy.

themes: leave them unto the poor and the stranger

schools.summary: The new word introducing the feast of trumpets — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (the new festival introduced by its own preface, marking it off as a distinct communication from the spring feasts, the autumn solemnities a fresh group, Ellicott); the turn in the year — the feasts now given falling in the seventh month, Tisri, which though seventh in the sacred reckoning begun with the Exodus had once been the first of the civil year, the old new year, so the seventh month opening with festal solemnity (Henry); the dense season — within its first three weeks the feast of trumpets, the day of atonement, and tabernacles, crowning the year's worship; God dividing his revelation, giving each feast its own word; the spring feasts looking back to redemption, the autumn feasts gathering up the year in repentance, atonement, and ingathering.

The feast of trumpets — 'in the first day of the month, shall ye have a sabbath... an holy convocation' (the first of Tisri, answering to September, beginning the civil year, kept as rest and sacred assembly, Clarke); 'a memorial of blowing of trumpets' (Hebrew 'remembrance blowing,' Num 29:1, the trumpets blown not in the common way of every new moon but extraordinarily throughout all the cities, Wesley); the trumpets calling the people to remembrance before God, summoning them to the solemn season of atonement at hand; the typology — the trumpet calling to repentance foreshadowing the gospel call and the last trumpet (1 Cor 15:52).

debates.question: What did the feast of trumpets commemorate or signify?

sides.position: a call to remembrance before God, summoning Israel to the solemn season of atonement at hand — the trumpet gathering the people and proclaiming God's acts
holders: the plain sense favoured by the witnesses

sides.position: the Jewish tradition that it commemorated Isaac's deliverance from the knife and the ram caught in the thicket
holders: the Jewish writers, recorded and marvelled at (rejected) by Calvin

themes: a memorial of blowing of trumpets
the first of the seventh month

schools.summary: The rest and offering of the feast of trumpets — 'ye shall do no servile work therein' (all ordinary handicraft, trade, and laborious work stopped, only the necessary preparing of food allowed, the day freed for worship, Gill/Ellicott); 'ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD' (the day honoured with special burnt and sin offerings, and since the first of the month was also the new moon, the sacrifices threefold — the daily continual, the new-moon, and the feast offering itself, Num 29:1, Ellicott); the trumpets and offerings calling Israel to draw near at the opening of the holiest month, preparing them for the day of atonement ten days off; rest and sacrifice joined, a summons to worship and self-examination before the great fast.

themes: preparing for the day of atonement

schools.summary: The new word introducing the day of atonement — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (the preface, recurring before each feast, distinguishing the day of atonement from the feast of trumpets, marking it its own ordinance, Gill/Ellicott); the place of this day — the only one of the appointed seasons that is a fast, not a feast, a day of affliction and humbling in the festal year, its full ritual given earlier (Lev 16:29) and here rehearsed for observance; the day of atonement at the heart of the autumn season, between the trumpets that summoned to repentance and the tabernacles that crowned the year with joy — first the call, then the cleansing, then the rejoicing; the typology — prefiguring the atoning work of Christ, who by his own blood entered once into the holy place (Heb 9:12).

themes: the one fast among the feasts
the call, the cleansing, the joy

schools.summary: The day of atonement and the afflicting of souls — 'on the tenth day of this seventh month... a holy convocation; and ye shall afflict your souls, and offer an offering made by fire' (the tenth of Tisri kept as a sacred assembly); 'afflict your souls' meaning fasting and self-humbling, the only fast in the calendar (Ellicott), a day of bitter repentance for all sins, especially the golden calf (Wesley); the sacrifice joined to the fast (Num 29:8), and by the fuller ritual of Lev 16 the high priest entering the holiest with blood; 'atonement' a Hebrew plural ('atonements'), signifying the appeasing of God and blotting out of guilt by expiation, the plural marking the fulness of the cleansing (Calvin); humbling within and atonement without meeting; the typology — the clearest figure of Christ's expiation.

themes: the tenth day of the seventh month

schools.summary: The entire rest of the day of atonement — 'ye shall do no work in that same day' (not merely abstinence from servile work as on festival days, but a complete rest from all manner of work, as the weekly Sabbath required, the day of atonement and the Sabbath alone kept with this entire ceasing, Ellicott/Gill); 'for it is a day of atonement, to make an atonement for you' (the day wholly given to expiation, the people doing nothing of their own, their rest answering to the truth that the atonement was God's work not theirs); signifying the necessity every man has of repentance and forgiveness, and the desperate state of the impenitent (Wesley); the full ritual given at Lev 16; the typology — resting while atonement is made figuring the believer resting from his own works to receive the finished atonement of Christ.

themes: to make an atonement for you

schools.summary: The penalty for not fasting — 'whatsoever soul... shall not be afflicted in that same day, he shall be cut off from among his people' (whoever did not humble himself and fast to be cut off, excluded from the covenant people, the divine excision of Lev 19:8, Ellicott); the limits — the law binding everyone who could fast and would not, the sick and children under nine excepted, the affliction required only of those able to bear it (Gill); the lesson — how greatly the sacrifice of an afflicted and humbled heart pleases God, since so heavy a punishment for the contempt of this humbling, God prizing the contrite spirit (Calvin); the outward fast standing for the inward humbling, to refuse it being to refuse repentance and despise the atonement, cutting oneself off from the people atoned.

themes: cut off from among his people
the fast bound on every soul

schools.summary: The penalty for working on the day — 'whatsoever soul... that doeth any work in that same day, the same soul will I destroy from among his people' (to do any work to incur destruction, 'any sort of work whatever,' the day kept as strictly as or more than the weekly Sabbath, Gill); the heightened language — whereas in most such breaches God threatens the offender shall be 'cut off,' here with peculiar force 'I will destroy him,' God himself undertaking the punishment, a sign of the singular sanctity of the day (Ellicott); both the fast (v29) and the rest (v30) guarded by the threat of destruction, that nothing profane the day; on this day, atonement being made for the nation, the people to do nothing but be still and be atoned for, to work being to thrust oneself out of the cleansing.

themes: I will destroy from among his people

schools.summary: The repeated and perpetual command of rest — 'ye shall do no manner of work: a statute for ever throughout your generations in all your dwellings' (the rule, already given vv28,30, stated again, repeated that it might be the more surely observed and to show how strictly God required the day kept, Gill); owing to the great sanctity of the day, the command repeated after the penalty to impress it the more effectually, the repetition a measure of the day's solemnity (Ellicott); the perpetuity and universality — binding all generations, not at the sanctuary only but in every dwelling; God laying great weight on this day's rest, guarding it with penalty and pressing it with repetition; the perpetual statute carrying the day down all Israel's history, the annual reminder of sin and its expiation.

The bounds of the day, evening to evening — 'a sabbath of rest, and ye shall afflict your souls' (the day at once a sabbath of total rest and a fast of afflicting the soul, rest and humbling joined, the body still and the heart bowed); 'in the ninth day at even, from even unto even' (beginning at the evening of the ninth and continuing till the evening of the tenth, a full day reckoned sunset to sunset after the Hebrew manner, the fast kept entire, Wesley); 'a sabbath of rest' the same phrase as Lev 16:31, marking its kinship with the weekly Sabbath in total rest (Ellicott); 'your sabbath,' peculiarly theirs, the great day of their atonement and rest; the precise bounding ensuring the whole was kept; the day of atonement the soul's sabbath, resting from sin in the atonement made.

The new word introducing the feast of tabernacles — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (the feast of tabernacles, like the feasts of new year and atonement, introduced by its own special preface, marking it a distinct ordinance and the last of the seventh-month solemnities, Keil and Delitzsch/Ellicott); the eminence — one of the three great pilgrim feasts at which all the males must appear, celebrated with more joy and gladness than any other, the most festal of all (Henry); its place — five days after the fast of atonement, turning the year's worship from humbling to rejoicing, the cleansing past; its double ground — the feast of ingathering at the year's end (Ex 23:16) and a memorial of the wilderness booths; the typology — God dwelling with his people in tents looking on to the Word made flesh who 'tabernacled' among us (John 1:14).

The feast of tabernacles set — 'the fifteenth day of this seventh month shall be the feast of tabernacles for seven days' (falling only four days after the day of atonement, the solemn fast swiftly followed by the gladdest feast, the cleansed people keeping festival, Ellicott); the people leaving their settled houses to dwell for the week in booths of branches, in the open air (Clarke, Wesley); the twofold meaning — the memorial sense, the booths reminding Israel their fathers dwelt in tents in the wilderness, kept and protected by God, stirring them to bless him (Calvin, Wesley); the other ground the ingathering of harvest; the typology — the booths figuring the pilgrim life of God's people, and God tabernacling with Israel looking on to Christ who 'dwelt' among us (John 1:14).

themes: the fifteenth day of the seventh month

schools.summary: The first day's holy convocation — 'on the first day shall be an holy convocation: ye shall do no servile work therein' (the people called together to holy exercises, prayer, praise, and the reading of the law, the joyful feast opening with worship, Gill); laborious work laid aside, the needful dressing of food allowed; the second-Temple observance, especially the water-pouring — at daybreak a priest with a jubilant procession and music went with a golden pitcher to the pool of Siloam, drew water, and poured it at the altar, a rite of thanksgiving and prayer for rain, to which our Lord alluded on 'the great day of the feast,' 'If any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink' (John 7:37, Ellicott); the gladdest feast no less framed by holy convocations, joy and worship together.

themes: the first day an holy convocation

schools.summary: The seven days' offerings and the eighth-day assembly — 'seven days ye shall offer an offering made by fire... on the eighth day shall be an holy convocation... a solemn assembly' (the sacrifices the most lavish of all — thirteen bullocks on the first day, with two rams, fourteen lambs, and a goat for sin, the bullocks decreasing by one each day, Num 29:13, Ellicott); they kept holiday on the first and eighth days, dwelling in booths the seven between (Calvin); the eighth day, though strictly a separate festival not one of the seven booth-days, belonging to the feast in a larger sense and called 'the great day of the feast,' on which our Lord cried the invitation to the thirsty (John 7:37, Wesley); the eighth day, beyond the seven, perhaps figuring the eternal rest beyond this world's pilgrimage.

themes: seven days an offering by fire
the eighth day a solemn assembly
the great day of the feast

schools.summary: The summary of the feasts — 'these are the feasts of the LORD... to offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD... every thing upon his day' (recapitulating the festivals summed up — Passover and unleavened bread, Pentecost, trumpets/new year, atonement, tabernacles — besides the weekly Sabbath, Ellicott/Gill); the common element — each feast having its own proper sacrifices, offered each on its appointed day, the worship of every season fixed and ordered, nothing left to man's choosing; the kinds of offering gathered under the term — burnt, meat, sacrifice (especially the sin offering, called by the general name because the chief end of all sacrifice was expiation, Wesley), drink; the feasts together rehearsing the whole gospel in shadow — redemption, firstfruits, the Spirit, atonement, God dwelling with his people.

themes: these are the feasts of the LORD

schools.summary: The feasts distinguished from the Sabbath and freewill gifts — 'beside the sabbaths of the LORD, and beside your gifts, and beside all your vows, and beside all your freewill offerings' (the feast offerings over and above the weekly Sabbath sacrifices, a feast falling on a Sabbath not setting aside the Sabbath's own observance, Gill); 'sabbaths' standing by metonymy for the sacrifices of the sabbaths (Ellicott); God not having any sabbath-sacrifice diminished because of the festival offerings, each rendered in full (Wesley); the feast offerings also distinct from the people's voluntary and occasional sacrifices — freewill gifts, vows, thank offerings — all continuing alongside the fixed festal worship; the appointed feasts not absorbing the ordinary and voluntary worship but added to it, God's due rendered in every kind.

themes: beside the sabbaths of the LORD

schools.summary: The feast of tabernacles as the ingathering — 'when ye have gathered in the fruit of the land, ye shall keep a feast unto the LORD seven days' (the feast falling after the whole produce of the year was gathered; the 'fruit' meaning not the corn long since reaped before Pentecost but the later fruits, the vintage and oil, the feast the harvest-home, Wesley); the law recurring to tabernacles after the summary of all the feasts (vv37-38), these closing verses supplementary, adding the reason and the rite of booths and branches (Ellicott, Keil); the double significance complete — both the ingathering, thanking God for the year's fruits, and the memorial of the wilderness booths; the typology — looking on to the great harvest-home at the end of the world when God's people are gathered in.

themes: when ye have gathered in the fruit
a feast unto the LORD seven days

schools.summary: The branches and the rejoicing — 'ye shall take you the boughs of goodly trees, branches of palm trees, and the boughs of thick trees, and willows of the brook; and ye shall rejoice before the LORD seven days'; the trees discussed — the 'goodly tree' commonly taken for the citron and the 'thick tree' for the myrtle (Clarke), or olive, myrtle, pine, and willow after Neh 8:15 (Wesley), the four species minutely defined in the second Temple (Ellicott); the branches serving to build and adorn the booths and carried in the festal processions; 'ye shall rejoice... seven days' — by the symbol of the green branches taught to keep the day with joy and gladness, the gladdest feast of the year (Calvin); the rejoicing commanded, a duty as well as a delight; the typology — the palms and rejoicing looking on to the multitude with palms before the throne (Rev 7:9).

themes: rejoice before the LORD seven days

schools.summary: The yearly perpetual keeping — 'ye shall keep it a feast unto the LORD seven days in the year. It shall be a statute for ever in your generations: ye shall celebrate it in the seventh month' (every year from the fifteenth to the twenty-second of Tisri, Gill); the 'seven days' denoting the feast of tabernacles proper (the booth-dwelling days), while the eighth day named earlier (Lev 23:36) was the separate concluding festival, the seven and the eighth making the full season (Ellicott); the perpetuity 'a statute for ever in your generations,' binding throughout the ages not that generation only; the fixed time, kept always in its appointed month; God securing the memory of his mercies by making the feast annual and perpetual, each generation owning the harvest and the wilderness keeping as its own.

themes: celebrate it in the seventh month
the feast made annual and lasting

schools.summary: The dwelling in booths — 'ye shall dwell in booths seven days; all that are Israelites born shall dwell in booths' (for the week the people left their houses and lodged in temporary booths, a duty on all the native-born); the booths set in their cities and towns, in the streets, gardens, or on the flat roofs, made of branches (Wesley); the dwelling for the seven days only, not the eighth, the eighth being a separate concluding festival when the booths were no longer used, Lev 23:36 (Ellicott, Gill); the meaning — by leaving settled homes for frail booths the people made to remember the pilgrim life of their fathers in the wilderness, owning their dwellings and the land as God's gift; the lesson — God's people pilgrims and strangers on the earth, their abiding home with God; the typology — God dwelling with his people in booths looking on to his dwelling with them for ever.

The reason of the feast — 'that your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in booths, when I brought them out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God' (the yearly keeping preserving, for children who never saw the desert, the memory of how their fathers dwelt in booths in the wilderness, with no settled home, kept by God alone); God's two objects — to perpetuate the remembrance of his providence and grace, both in bringing Israel out of Egypt and in preserving them through the wilderness (Clarke); the contrast giving the feast its force — when the posterity securely settled in Canaan amid plenty, the yearly booth reminding them all they had was of God's goodness (Ellicott); 'I am the LORD your God,' grounded in his covenant authority; God would have his mercies remembered across the generations.

themes: I made Israel dwell in booths
when I brought them out of Egypt
the memory of the wilderness preserved

schools.summary: Moses' faithful declaring of the feasts — 'Moses declared unto the children of Israel the feasts of the LORD' (in accordance with the charge received at the chapter's opening, Lev 23:2, making known the number, order, manner, time, and motive of the festivals, a fitting conclusion, Ellicott); setting before them the several feasts recited — Passover and unleavened bread, firstfruits, Pentecost, trumpets, atonement, tabernacles — with their order, way, and season (Gill); the faithfulness of the mediator, declaring God's whole message and holding nothing back; Wesley's thankful reflection — God's people now bound to feasts fewer, less burdensome, more spiritual and significant, surer and sweeter earnests of the rest to come, the shadows given way to the substance in Christ; all the feasts together rehearsing in shadow the work of redemption fulfilled in Christ.

themes: Moses declared the feasts of the LORD
